quiz_questions.question_text: The COBE satellite was significant because it:

options.text: Discovered Pluto
Mapped the Milky Way in visible light
First detected the tiny temperature fluctuations in the CMB
Found the first exoplanet

quiz_questions.explanation: COBE (1989-1993) confirmed the CMB's blackbody spectrum and detected anisotropies — earning Mather and Smoot a Nobel Prize.

quiz_questions.question_text: WMAP improved on COBE by:

options.text: Discovering dark energy
Providing a much more detailed map of CMB temperature fluctuations
Landing on the Moon
Photographing black holes

quiz_questions.explanation: WMAP (2001-2010) provided precise measurements of cosmological parameters from the CMB.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Planck satellite provided:

options.text: A new theory of gravity
The first photograph of a star
Evidence that the Earth is flat
The most precise full-sky CMB map, tightly constraining cosmological parameters

quiz_questions.explanation: ESA's Planck mission (2009-2013) measured the CMB with unprecedented precision.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Hubble Deep Field image revealed:

options.text: A detailed high-resolution image of the Sun's photosphere showing granulation patterns, sunspots, and coronal mass ejections
A catalog of several thousand bright stellar objects located within a few hundred light-years of our solar system in the local neighborhood
A completely blank field with no detectable sources of any kind, confirming that certain regions of the sky are truly devoid of objects
Thousands of galaxies in a tiny patch of seemingly empty sky, many billions of light-years away

quiz_questions.explanation: The 1995 Hubble Deep Field showed that even the 'emptiest' sky is filled with distant galaxies.

quiz_questions.question_text: The discovery of the accelerating expansion in 1998 used:

options.text: Star positions
Distant Type Ia supernovae as standard candles
Radar measurements
Radio waves from pulsars

quiz_questions.explanation: Two teams (Supernova Cosmology Project and High-z SN Search Team) independently discovered the acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Gravitational wave detection by LIGO confirmed:

options.text: The direct measurement and confirmation of dark energy as a physical substance permeating all of intergalactic space uniformly
Einstein's general relativity prediction of spacetime ripples from merging massive objects
The fundamental emptiness of the vacuum of space, demonstrating that no matter or energy exists between astronomical objects anywhere
The classical Newtonian framework of gravitational attraction between massive bodies acting instantaneously at a distance through space

quiz_questions.explanation: LIGO's 2015 detection of merging black holes opened a new window on the universe and confirmed a key GR prediction.

quiz_questions.question_text: The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) is designed to:

options.text: Observe the earliest galaxies in infrared and study planet formation
Create comprehensive high-resolution topographic maps of all terrestrial landmasses and ocean floor features using orbital remote sensing
Probe the internal nuclear fusion processes occurring deep within the solar interior using advanced helioseismology measurement techniques
Serve as next-generation global positioning system satellites providing enhanced navigation accuracy for civilian and military applications

quiz_questions.explanation: JWST's infrared capabilities allow it to see the most distant, redshifted galaxies and peer through dust.

quiz_questions.question_text: Baryon acoustic oscillations (BAOs) are:

options.text: A 'standard ruler' in the distribution of galaxies, tracing sound waves from the early universe
Quantum mechanical oscillations of individual atoms and molecules driven by thermal energy and electromagnetic interactions at the atomic scale
Seismic-type disturbances propagating through the fabric of spacetime, analogous to geological tremors occurring within planetary interiors
Audible acoustic frequencies generated during the initial moments of the universe that continue resonating throughout the cosmos today

quiz_questions.explanation: BAOs left a characteristic ~500 million light-year imprint in galaxy distributions, useful for measuring cosmic expansion.

quiz_questions.question_text: The discovery of cosmic neutrino background is:

options.text: The cosmic neutrino background has been directly detected and fully characterized by multiple independent particle physics experiments worldwide
Predicted but not yet directly detected from the Big Bang (though supernova neutrinos have been detected)
No theoretical framework in modern physics or cosmology predicts the existence of a primordial neutrino background radiation from the early universe
Fundamental physical principles and experimental results have definitively demonstrated that a cosmic neutrino background cannot possibly exist

quiz_questions.explanation: A cosmic neutrino background similar to the CMB should exist from ~1 second after the Big Bang, but it's incredibly hard to detect.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory will:

options.text: Conduct comprehensive bathymetric surveys of all ocean basins and coastal regions using satellite-based remote sensing altimetry instruments
Serve as the direct successor and replacement for the James Webb Space Telescope in conducting deep infrared observations of the cosmos
Survey the entire visible sky repeatedly, discovering billions of objects and mapping dark matter/energy
Function exclusively as a dedicated solar observatory monitoring the Sun's activity cycles, flares, and coronal mass ejection events continuously

quiz_questions.explanation: Named after the dark matter pioneer, the Rubin Observatory will enable unprecedented time-domain and cosmological surveys.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the CMB power spectrum?

options.text: The brightness of the CMB at different times
A radio frequency chart
A power plant in space
A plot showing the intensity of CMB temperature fluctuations at different angular scales

quiz_questions.explanation: The CMB power spectrum reveals the contributions of baryonic matter, dark matter, and dark energy through acoustic peak patterns.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a standard candle?

options.text: A star of standard size
An astronomical object with a known intrinsic luminosity, used for distance measurement
A regular candle used in observatories
A calibration tool for telescopes

quiz_questions.explanation: Type Ia supernovae and Cepheid variables are standard candles — their known luminosities enable distance estimation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the cosmic distance ladder?

options.text: One specific observational method using a single type of instrument that can measure distances to all astronomical objects accurately
A series of overlapping distance measurement methods, from nearby (parallax) to distant (supernovae)
A material structure extending from Earth's surface into outer space that enables direct physical access to nearby stellar objects above us
An ordered catalog that lists all known galaxies from nearest to farthest based on their measured angular sizes in the sky

quiz_questions.explanation: Each rung of the distance ladder calibrates the next: parallax → Cepheids → Type Ia SNe → Hubble's Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is Big Bang nucleosynthesis?

options.text: The creation of heavy elements in supernovae
Building atoms from scratch
Nuclear reactions in modern stars
The formation of light elements (H, He, Li) in the first few minutes after the Big Bang

quiz_questions.explanation: BBN correctly predicts the abundances of hydrogen (~75%), helium (~25%), and trace lithium in the universe.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are baryon acoustic oscillations (BAOs)?

options.text: Periodic fluctuations in the density of visible matter left by sound waves in the early universe
Acoustic vibrations currently being produced by baryonic matter throughout the present-day universe at detectable frequency ranges
Oscillatory disturbances propagating through the dark matter component of the universe, driven by gravitational self-interaction forces
The standing wave patterns formed on tensioned strings of musical instruments, governed by classical mechanics and harmonic theory

quiz_questions.explanation: BAOs provide a cosmic 'standard ruler' for measuring the expansion history of the universe.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the cosmological principle?

options.text: A principle of telescope design
The assumption that on large scales, the universe is homogeneous and isotropic
That Earth is the center of everything
That the cosmos must have planets

quiz_questions.explanation: The cosmological principle is supported by the CMB's uniformity and the large-scale distribution of galaxies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the ΛCDM model?

options.text: The standard model of cosmology incorporating dark energy (Λ), cold dark matter (CDM), and ordinary matter
A high-energy physics experiment conducted at facilities like CERN that collides subatomic particles to probe fundamental force interactions
A light-emitting diode technology used in astronomical instrumentation for calibrating detector sensitivity across different wavelength bands
A specific design configuration for astronomical telescopes that optimizes light-gathering power and angular resolution for deep sky observations

quiz_questions.explanation: ΛCDM successfully explains the CMB, large-scale structure, expansion history, and element abundances.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is look-back time?

options.text: The age of a star
The time since the Big Bang
The time it took light from a distant object to reach us
How far back a telescope can zoom

quiz_questions.explanation: A galaxy at a look-back time of 10 billion years is seen as it was 10 billion years ago.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is recombination?

options.text: A controlled reaction conducted in a chemistry laboratory where molecular compounds are separated and reformed under specific temperature conditions
The gravitational merger of two separate galaxy systems into a single larger structure over billions of years of dynamical interaction in space
The epoch ~380,000 years after the Big Bang when electrons combined with nuclei, releasing the CMB
The continuous cycle of stellar material being expelled by dying stars and then reincorporated into new generations of forming stellar objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Despite the name, this was the first time atoms formed; the universe became transparent to light, releasing the CMB.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the dark ages?

options.text: The period between the CMB release and the formation of the first stars, when no visible light sources existed
The era in European history roughly spanning the fifth through fifteenth centuries when astronomical knowledge and observation stagnated significantly
The regular period of darkness experienced on the side of our planet facing away from the Sun during its twenty-four-hour axial rotation cycle
A hypothetical cosmological epoch during which dark matter was temporarily absent from the universe due to some unknown physical mechanism

quiz_questions.explanation: The cosmic dark ages ended with the epoch of reionization, when the first stars and galaxies lit up.

quiz_questions.question_text: CMB data shows a specific pattern of peaks in its power spectrum. What do the positions of these peaks reveal?

options.text: The total count of individual galaxy systems present within the observable universe, estimated at several hundred billion to two trillion objects
The surface and core temperature values of our nearest star, measured through spectroscopic analysis and helioseismological modeling techniques
The total elapsed time since the formation of our planet from the solar nebula, determined through radiometric dating of ancient mineral samples
The geometry of the universe (flat, open, or closed) and the density of various components

quiz_questions.explanation: The first peak position confirms a flat universe; peak heights constrain dark matter and baryonic matter densities.

quiz_questions.question_text: JWST discovers galaxies at z > 10 that appear surprisingly well-formed. Why is this significant?

options.text: JWST can't observe that far
Galaxies can't exist at z > 10
It's not significant
It suggests galaxy formation began earlier than some models predicted

quiz_questions.explanation: Early, massive galaxies challenge some structure formation models and constrain the conditions of the very early universe.

quiz_questions.question_text: A supernova survey finds that 1998's accelerating expansion result holds up after 25+ years of data. What has this confirmed?

options.text: The measurements were wrong all along
Dark energy doesn't exist
Dark energy is a robust cosmological component, not a measurement artifact
The universe is static

quiz_questions.explanation: Consistent results from multiple independent teams and methods have firmly established dark energy's reality.

quiz_questions.question_text: Gravitational waves from a neutron star merger are detected simultaneously with electromagnetic signals. What is this called?

options.text: Multi-messenger astronomy
Double detection
A false alarm
Dual-wave theory

quiz_questions.explanation: GW170817 inaugurated multi-messenger astronomy, using both gravitational waves and light to study the same event.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the CMB were perfectly uniform (no fluctuations), what would this imply about cosmic structure?

options.text: The universe would be the same as we see it
Dark matter wouldn't exist
Stars would still form easily
Galaxies and large-scale structure could not have formed through gravitational growth

quiz_questions.explanation: Without density fluctuations, gravity would have nothing to amplify into the galaxies and clusters we see.

quiz_questions.question_text: BAO measurements at different redshifts map the expansion rate over time. What do these measurements constrain?

options.text: The equation of state of dark energy (whether it is constant or changing)
The total population count of luminous stellar objects in the observable universe, estimated at roughly two hundred sextillion individual stars
Exclusively the present-day value of the expansion rate, providing no information about how the expansion has changed over cosmic history
The elapsed time since our central star first ignited hydrogen fusion in its core approximately four point six billion years ago in the solar nebula

quiz_questions.explanation: Comparing BAO scales at different redshifts reveals how the expansion rate changed, constraining dark energy properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Hubble Deep Field was observed by pointing at a seemingly empty region. Why was this approach chosen?

options.text: Sky regions containing numerous bright nearby stars and galaxies offer no scientifically interesting targets for deep cosmological investigation purposes
To avoid bright nearby objects and reveal the ultra-faint, distant galaxy population
The selection of that particular empty sky region was an unintentional error by the observing team that fortunately produced unexpected scientific results
All other regions of the sky had already been thoroughly surveyed, leaving only this seemingly vacant patch as the remaining available target field

quiz_questions.explanation: The empty region maximized sensitivity to the most distant, faintest galaxies without contamination from nearby bright sources.

quiz_questions.question_text: Predicted elemental abundances from Big Bang nucleosynthesis match observations of primordial gas clouds. Why is this test powerful?

options.text: It independently confirms the Big Bang model's predictions about the first 20 minutes of the universe
The primary value of nucleosynthesis predictions lies in calibrating and validating the measurement precision of modern astronomical instrumentation
Big Bang nucleosynthesis theory generates predictions for only a single chemical element, severely limiting its usefulness as a cosmological test
The agreement between predicted and observed elemental abundances is considered weak and inconclusive by most cosmologists studying the early universe

quiz_questions.explanation: BBN predicts specific H/He/Li ratios; their agreement with observations is a pillar of Big Bang cosmology.

quiz_questions.question_text: The first direct image of a black hole's shadow was taken by the EHT in 2019. How was this achieved?

options.text: By linking radio telescopes worldwide to create an Earth-sized virtual telescope
By pointing Hubble at M87
By flying a camera into the black hole
With a single large radio dish

quiz_questions.explanation: Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) combined signals from globally distributed telescopes to achieve the needed resolution.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the universe is only 13.8 billion years old, why can we see objects 46 billion light-years away?

options.text: Light travels faster than we think
Time moves differently at the edge of the universe
Space has been expanding while light traveled, carrying distant objects far beyond 13.8 billion light-years
We can't actually see that far

quiz_questions.explanation: The expansion of space during the light's journey means objects are now much farther than the light-travel distance.
